breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new of IQueryable v, then v.Skip().Take().ToList() (L380) + v.Count() (L381); no AsEnumerable boundary before projection. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/MyReportsController.cs at line 372, replace the untranslatable shim call with a direct EF-translatable DateTime.AddDays so it emits SQL DATEADD (mirroring legacy DbFunctions.AddDays). Because QuotDate is non-nullable DateTime, do NOT use `.Value`.

Replace:
  validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.QuotDate, (b.QuotValidity == null) ? 0 : b.QuotValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

With:
  validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.QuotDate.AddDays((b.QuotValidity == null) ? 0 : b.QuotValidity.Value + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

This preserves the original output exactly (null validity -> +0 days; otherwise +(validity+1) days; Active if now <= that date, else Expired) and EF Core 10 translates `b.QuotDate.AddDays(n)` to DATEADD(day, n, QuotDate).

Note: the identical broken pattern (DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays on a Quotation/PurchaseQuotation date in a server-side projection) appears in Models/Common.cs:9931 (QuotDate) and Common.cs:10817 (PQuotDate), and likely the QuotationController quotation grid. Those should get the same fix (use b.QuotDate.AddDays / b.PQuotDate.AddDays since both date columns are non-nullable DateTime). The commented-out lines at Common.cs:10198/10410/10624 are inert and need no change.
reasoning:
I attempted to refute but the claim holds â€” this is a genuine EF Core 10 port regression that 500s.

SERVER-SIDE confirmed. The query `v` is built directly off the DbSet at Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:328 (`var v = (from b in db.Quotations join ... select new {...})`) with a long chain of LINQ joins and a `select new` projection at L352. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.ToArray()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary anywhere before the projection â€” the materialization boundaries are AFTER the projection: `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()` (L380) and `v.Count()` (L381). So the `validity = ...` expression at L372 is composed into the server-side IQueryable and gets translated to SQL. It is not a standalone top-level query and not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping.

WHY IT 500s. The projection contains `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.QuotDate, (b.QuotValidity == null) ? 0 : b.QuotValidity + 1)`. `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is NOT an EF-registered/canonical function â€” it is a plain CLR helper defined in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70-71:
  public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);
  public static DateTime AddDays(DateTime date, int days) => date.AddDays(days);
With first arg `b.QuotDate` (non-nullable DateTime per Models/Quotation.cs:13) and second arg `b.QuotValidity + 1` (an `int?`, since QuotValidity is `int?` per Quotation.cs:43), overload resolution selects the `(DateTime?, int?)` overload returning `DateTime?`. EF Core 10 has no way to translate this arbitrary static CLR method into SQL, so it throws InvalidOperationException ("The LINQ expression ... could not be translated") at enumeration (L380) -> HTTP 500. Unlike a true `g.Select(...).FirstOrDefault()` edge case, this throws on EVERY load of the grid regardless of data (the untranslatable node is always present), so severity = HIGH.

PORT REGRESSION confirmed against legacy. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:378 used the canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.QuotDate, ...)`, which EF6 translates to SQL DATEADD and runs fine. The port mechanically swapped `DbFunctions.AddDays` -> `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` (the shim), breaking translation. This is the exact same class of swap seen across the codebase.

One correction to the hunter's proposed fix: QuotDate is a non-nullable `DateTime`, so `b.QuotDate.Value` does NOT compile (`.Value` only exists on `DateTime?`). The correct EF-translatable fix calls `.AddDays(...)` directly on `b.QuotDate`. `DateTime.AddDays(double)` IS translated by EF Core to DATEADD. The day count must be a concrete non-null value, so use `(b.QuotValidity ?? -1) + 1` (or `(b.QuotValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.QuotValidity.Value + 1)`) to keep the exact legacy semantics: validity==null -> +0 days, else +(validity+1) days.

Because PQuotDate is a non-nullable DateTime, do NOT use `.Value`. Because PQuotValidity is int?, coalesce it to a concrete int before AddDays.

With:
    validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.PQuotDate.AddDays((b.PQuotValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.PQuotValidity.Value + 1))) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Equivalent (preserving the original comparison direction and Active/Expired semantics). `b.PQuotDate.AddDays(int)` translates to DATEADD(day, n, PQuotDate); the null-branch yields 0 (no shift) exactly as before; the non-null branch shifts by PQuotValidity+1, identical to legacy. Output is byte-for-byte preserved. Note the hunter's suggested `b.PQuotDate.Value.AddDays(...)` will NOT compile since PQuotDate is non-nullable â€” drop `.Value`.
reasoning:
I could not refute this; it is a genuine EF Core 10 port-break. Facts verified against the actual code:

SERVER-SIDE: Confirmed. Controllers/MyReportsController.cs lines 458-498 build `v` as a pure IQueryable over db.PurchaseQuotations with five left joins. The `validity` expression is inside the `select new` (line 495). The first materialization is line 500 `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()` and line 501 `v.Count()`. There is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before the projection, so the entire `select new` (including `validity`) must be translated to SQL. Not a standalone top-level query, not a group-aggregate. Genuinely server-side.

PORT REGRESSION: Confirmed. Legacy _archive\legacy-mvc5\Controllers\MyReportsController.cs:502 has the identical expression using `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.PQuotDate, ...)` â€” EF6's canonical function which translates to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays`, defined in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70 as `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);` â€” a plain unregistered CLR static method, NOT an EF.Functions/HasDbFunction-registered method. The class comment itself (SendMailShim.cs:67) admits "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated."

WHY IT 500s: EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary static CLR method call (`DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays`) embedded in a server-side projection. At query compilation (triggered by .ToList() on line 500) it throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated." This propagates as an unhandled HTTP 500. The failure is at translation time, so it fires regardless of data volume â€” high severity (will 500 on every realistic request to this report).

Entity types verified: Models/PurchaseQuotation.cs:16 `public DateTime PQuotDate` (NON-nullable), line 44 `public int? PQuotValidity` (nullable). This matters for the fix: the hunter's proposed `b.PQuotDate.Value.AddDays(...)` is INCORRECT because PQuotDate is a non-nullable DateTime and has no `.Value` property â€” that would be a compile error. The fix must drop `.Value`.

Verdict = confirmed: it is server-side AND it throws a runtime 500 in EF Core 10. The hunter's diagnosis is right; only its proposed fix has the `.Value` defect, which I correct in finalFix.

finalFix:
In Controllers/MyReportsController.cs at line 1464, replace:

    validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.DvDate, (b.DvValidity == null) ? 0 : b.DvValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

with the EF-native form (translates to SQL DATEADD; note DvDate is non-nullable DateTime so NO `.Value` on it):

    validity = (b.DvDate.AddDays((b.DvValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.DvValidity + 1).Value) >= DateTime.Now) ? "Active" : "Expired",

`b.DvDate.AddDays(int)` translates to `DATEADD(day, N, DvDate)` server-side; the comparison to `DateTime.Now` translates to a parameterized comparison, mirroring legacy EF6 `DbFunctions.AddDays` semantics exactly. (The non-nullable `int` day arg avoids the `int?` overload that would re-introduce a nullable-cast EF can still handle but is unnecessary here.) This same class of break exists at the sibling Quotation/PQuotation/PO validity lines (e.g. MyReportsController.cs ~378, ~502, ~3733) and in CustomerRelationController/HomeController/DeliverynoteController â€” apply the identical pattern there, adjusting `.Value` only where the *Date column is itself nullable.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED â€” the hunter is correct; I could not refute it. Location: Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:1464, inside the delivery-note report grid method (anonymous projection `select new { ... }` of the IQueryable `v` built at lines 1422-1469). 

(a) SERVER-SIDE: The projection is part of IQueryable `v`. The LINQ chain from `db.Deliverynotes` through the joins, `where`, `select new`, and `.OrderBy` has NO `.ToList()`/`.ToArray()`/`.AsEnumerable()` boundary. Materialization happens only later at line 1471 (`v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()`) and line 1472 (`v.Count()`). So the `validity` expression is composed into a server-evaluated query. The sibling line 1460 (`db.Users.Where(...).Select(...).FirstOrDefault()`) is a correlated server subquery, confirming EF is translating the whole projection to SQL, not client-evaluating it.

(b) WOULD 500: `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain static CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (lines 70-71), explicitly commented "Compat for EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays ... client-evaluated." It is NOT registered as an EF function â€” a repo-wide search found no `HasDbFunction`/`[DbFunction]` mapping for it. Overload resolution: arg1 `b.DvDate` is non-nullable DateTime, arg2 `(b.DvValidity==null)?0:b.DvValidity+1` is `int?` (since `DvValidity` is `int?`, line 41 of Deliverynote.cs, and `+1` lifts to `int?`), so the selected overload is `AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days)` returning `DateTime?` â€” body `date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`. EF Core 10 cannot translate this arbitrary CLR method to SQL DATEADD, so executing the query throws InvalidOperationException 'could not be translated' â†’ HTTP 500. This fails on the FIRST execution regardless of data (not edge-dependent), hence HIGH severity.

(c) PORT REGRESSION: Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:1520 used `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.DvDate, ...)`, the canonical EF6 function that translates to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in the non-translatable `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays`. Confirmed regression, not a pre-existing legacy bug.

Note on the proposed fix: it uses `b.DvDate.Value.AddDays(...)`, but `DvDate` is a non-nullable `DateTime` (Deliverynote.cs line 13), so `.Value` would not compile. The correct EF-native form omits `.Value` on `DvDate`. The validity-day argument must be a non-nullable `int` for the EF-native `DateTime.AddDays(double)` overload to translate cleanly; `(b.DvValidity + 1).Value` resolves to `int` after the null check. Semantics preserved: legacy `Now <= DvDate + N days` is logically identical to `DvDate + N days >= Now`.

finalFix:
Replace line 3652 in Controllers/MyReportsController.cs with an EF-native AddDays on the column (translates to SQL DATEADD), mirroring legacy EF6 semantics (DateTime.Now <= addedDate, i.e. addedDate >= DateTime.Now):

validity = (a.PODate.AddDays((a.POValidity == null) ? 0 : (a.POValidity.Value + 1)) >= DateTime.Now) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Notes: PODate is non-nullable DateTime so use a.PODate.AddDays(...) WITHOUT .Value (the hunter's .Value would not compile). POValidity is int?, so use a.POValidity.Value + 1 in the non-null branch (or (int)a.POValidity + 1). This generates DATEADD(day, CASE WHEN POValidity IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE POValidity + 1 END, PODate) >= GETDATE(), exactly preserving the legacy "Active"/"Expired" output. The same pattern (DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays in a server-side projection) also appears at SalesOrder's GetSalesOrder (SODate/SOValidity, ~line matching legacy 3893) and the Quotation/PQuotation/Delivery grids â€” fix those identically (drop .Value on non-nullable date columns, unwrap the nullable *Validity int?).
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the hunter's flag at Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:3652, inside method GetPurchaseOrder (POST). The fragment is genuinely SERVER-SIDE: the query `var v = (from a in db.PurchaseOrders join ... select new { ..., validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.PODate, (a.POValidity == null) ? 0 : a.POValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired", ... }).OrderBy(...)` (lines 3622-3654) is a pure IQueryable with NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary in the chain. It is first enumerated at line 3656 `var data = v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList();` and again at line 3657 `recordsTotal = v.Count();`. Both force the projection (including the validity expression) to be translated to SQL.

DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain static CLR helper, not a registered EF function. Verified at Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:68-76 â€” `public static class DbFunctionsCompat` with method bodies `AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);` and `AddDays(DateTime date, int days) => date.AddDays(days);`. The file's own comment (line 67) says "client-evaluated". It is not wired via modelBuilder.HasDbFunction or [DbFunction], so EF Core 10's translator has no SQL mapping for it. When it appears in a server-side projection, EF Core 10 throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. EF Core 10 does NOT silently client-evaluate a method call that mixes a column expression (a.PODate) in a projection; arbitrary client-eval in projections was removed in EF Core 3.0. So this 500s on ANY non-empty result set = high severity, realistic data.

PORT REGRESSION confirmed by side-by-side: legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MyReportsController.cs:3733 used `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.PODate, ...)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that maps to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays, breaking translation. The app worked in EF6.

Entity types verified at Models/PurchaseOrder.cs: PODate is non-nullable `DateTime` (line 17); POValidity is `int?` (line 46).

One correction to the hunter's proposed fix: it wrote `a.PODate.Value.AddDays(...)`, but PODate is a non-nullable DateTime, so `.Value` does not exist and would be a COMPILE error. The correct EF-native fix drops `.Value` on PODate and unwraps the nullable POValidity.

reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real EF Core 10 port-break (high severity), though the hunter's proposed fix has a compile error I correct below.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: yes. In Controllers/MyReportsController.cs method GetSalesOrder (POST, starts L3725), `v` is one unbroken LINQ-to-Entities IQueryable: `var v = (from a in db.SalesOrders ... select new {... validity = ...} ).OrderBy(a=>a.SODate).ThenBy(a=>a.SOCreatedDate);` (L3771-3816). There is NO .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable() boundary anywhere before the projection. `v` is first enumerated at L3818 `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()` and again at L3819 `v.Count()`. So the `validity` expression at L3810 is translated to SQL. Not a standalone client query, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping.

(b) WOULD 500: yes, unconditionally on any realistic data. The flagged expression (L3810) is `validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.SODate, (a.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : a.SOValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired"`. `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain static CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L68-71: `public static DateTime AddDays(DateTime date, int days) => date.AddDays(days);`), with no [DbFunction] attribute / HasDbFunction registration â€” the file comment literally says "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated." EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary user CLR method call embedded in a server-side projection, so it throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. This is not edge-data dependent; it fires the moment the query executes.

PORT REGRESSION: yes. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs:9757 used the canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.SODate, (b.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : b.SOValidity + 1)`, which EF6 translated to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in the untranslatable CLR shim.

Entity types confirmed (Models/SalesOrder.cs): SODate is non-nullable `DateTime` (L13); SOValidity is `int?` (L41). Therefore the hunter's proposed fix `a.SODate.Value.AddDays(...)` does NOT compile â€” `.Value` is invalid on a non-nullable DateTime. The correct EF-native form omits `.Value` on SODate and unwraps the int? day count.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: b=GeneratedItems left join; GeneratedItems.Qty/Price/Amount are non-nullable. If a production has no generated-item row, b is NULL and the unguarded reads + multiplications (lines 8323-8329) read NULL into non-nullable -> EF Core 10 500. Server-side. Legacy identical. MED: only when a production row lacks generated items.
finalFix:
Null-guard the left-joined `b` entity inside the server-side projection so EF Core 10 emits SQL that yields 0 instead of reading NULL into non-nullable decimal â€” reproducing EF6's default(decimal)=0 behavior exactly. Replace lines 8323-8329:

    b.Qty,
    b.Price,
    b.Amount,
    singlepcost= d.Labourcost,
    productioncost =d.Expense *b.Qty,
    labourcost=d.Labourcost*b.Qty,
    materialcost=d.meterialcost *b.Qty,

with (use conditional reads that translate to SQL CASE/COALESCE and keep arithmetic null-tolerant):

    Qty = b != null ? b.Qty : 0,
    Price = b != null ? b.Price : 0,
    Amount = b != null ? b.Amount : 0,
    singlepcost = d.Labourcost,
    productioncost = d.Expense * (b != null ? b.Qty : 0),
    labourcost = d.Labourcost * (b != null ? b.Qty : 0),
    materialcost = d.meterialcost * (b != null ? b.Qty : 0),

This preserves output: EF6 produced Qty/Price/Amount=0 and the *cost products=0 (since b.Qty coerced to 0) for orphan rows; the guarded version yields identical values. d.Expense/Labourcost/meterialcost remain decimal? (their own NULLs already propagate as in EF6, producing null cost members, matching legacy). No change needed to c.ItemName/d.BOMName (string, NULL-safe) or the downstream client-side Select/Distinct/OrderBy. The `b != null` translates to a SQL key-null check on the left join, which EF Core 10 supports.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED (could not refute). File QuickNetCore/Controllers/MyReportsController.cs, method GetProductionSummary (lines 8285-8360), projection at lines 8316-8329, flagged reads 8323-8329.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” verified: The `select new {...}` projection containing b.Qty/b.Price/b.Amount and the d.Expense*b.Qty / d.Labourcost*b.Qty / d.meterialcost*b.Qty expressions is the LAST operator composed into the IQueryable BEFORE the materialization boundary. The `.AsEnumerable()` on line 8330 is applied to the *result* of `})` â€” it pulls the DOWNSTREAM `.Select(o=>new{...}).Distinct().OrderBy(...)` (8330-8345) client-side, but NOT the projection itself, which is translated to SQL and executed server-side when `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()` (8347) and `v.Count()` (8348) enumerate. So the flagged fragment is genuinely server-side; it is NOT after the boundary, not a standalone top-level local, not a group-aggregate.

(b) WOULD 500 on edge data â€” verified: `b` = GeneratedItems (db.GeneratedItem), left-joined via `join...into payf / from b in payf.DefaultIfEmpty()` (8305-8306), so b can be SQL NULL. GeneratedItems.Qty/Price/Amount are non-nullable `decimal` (Models/Production.cs lines 56,59,61). The `where` (8311-8312) only constrains b.BOM/b.Item when the optional BOM/generated params are non-zero, so in the default 'all' case a Production header with zero GeneratedItems rows survives with b=NULL. EF Core 10's relational shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR decimal â†’ InvalidOperationException ('The data is NULL at ordinal... Use a nullable type') at enumeration â†’ HTTP 500. EF6 silently coerced to default(decimal)=0.

PORT REGRESSION â€” verified: legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MyReportsController.cs (method at line 8665, projection at 8696-8709) is byte-identical except DbFunctions.DiffDaysâ†’EF.Functions.DateDiffDay (irrelevant). EF6 tolerated the NULL left-join value reads; EF Core 10 does not.

Note d (BillOfMaterials) Expense/Labourcost/meterialcost are already decimal? so d-side NULLs are safe; only the b.Qty/Price/Amount reads and the `* b.Qty` factors throw. SEVERITY: MED â€” fires only when a production row has no generated-item row (in-progress/voided/detail-less header), not the common populated path.
